original_abstract: This chapter examines the stark disconnect between the rhetoric of public schooling—which champions democracy and equitable social relations—and the reality of anti-violence and conflict management initiatives implemented in typical urban Canadian schools. Based on a qualitative study, the findings reveal that despite a few notable exceptions, school practices overwhelmingly prioritize exclusionary "peacekeeping" characterized by surveillance, control, and inherent gender and racial biases. This focus on stability and compliance far outweighs any commitment to sustainable, democratic peacebuilding. The study suggests that systemic budget cuts and curriculum standardization have further entrenched these patterns. Ultimately, the chapter probes how such narrow approaches to "peace" education may inadvertently reinforce racialized social inequalities and perpetuate the very cycles of violence they claim to prevent.
humanized_abstract: Chapter 4 Schooling, “peace,” and discipline The preceding chapters have described the theory and methods used in this study. In this chapter, we reveal the enormous gap between the discourse surrounding schooling and the discourse of anti-violence and conflict resolution activities practised in the everyday of the typical urban Canadian school. While there were a few unusual exceptions, this study revealed that the dominant practice of peacekeeping in our schools was characterized by practices of discipline, punishment, surveillance and gender and racial inequality and that these practices were firmly entrenched, at least in part because of the impacts of austerity measures and common core curriculum. This chapter examines how schooling as discipline can stifle any meaningful discourse of peace that could be truly democratic and social in nature, and the ways in which current practices of discipline and enforcement may perpetuate and even deepen racial inequality, which is a direct barrier to peace.
